There has been a renewed focus on efficiency techniques (Gale et al., 2019; Ogueji et al., 2022; Ahia et al., 2021). Quantization as a form of model compression of large language models has become increasingly 29relevant as a way to minimize memory requirements and minimize compute intensity (Dettmers et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022; Frantar et al., 2022; Park et al., 2022a; Kim et al.). ModelEfficiencyatInferenceTime Researchinmodelcompressionmostlyfallsinthecategories of quantization techniques (Jacob et al., 2018; Courbariaux et al., 2014; Hubara et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2015), efforts to start with a network that is more compact with fewer parameters, layers or computations (architecture design) (Howard et al., 2017; Iandola et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017), student networks with fewer parameters that learn from a larger teacher model (model distillation) (Hinton et al., 2015) and finally pruning by setting a subset of weights or filters to zero (Louizos et al., 2017; Wen et al., 2016; LeCun et al., 1990; Hassibi et al., 1993a; StrГ¶m, 1997; Hassibi et al., 1993b; See et al., 2016; Narang et al., 2017; Frantar & Alistarh, 2023; Sanh et al., 2020). Often, a combination of compression methods might be applied. For example, pruning might be combined with other efficiency-improving methods, e.g. quantization or faster search algorithms. Quantization TechniquesQuantization can be used to speed up inference and relax hardware requirements, as has been shown for e.g., 8-bit (Quinn & Ballesteros, 2018), 4-bit (Aji & Heafield, 2020) and recently also below 3-bit quantization (Park et al., 2022a) of neural machine translation models. Park et al. (2022a) utilize non-uniform quantization to achieve high compression ratios. However, this method requires the use of specialized kernels for compressed (2-bit/4-bit) weights and floating point activations and involve finding the binary representations using expensive iterative search or QAT. Similar to Park et al. (2022a), Frantar et al. (2022) also demonstrate compression of model parameters to 3 or 4-bit precision allowing inference off a single A100 GPU. However, they also perform weight-only quantization limiting the speedup as the activations are kept at higher precision (FP16). Quantization reduces the number of bits needed to represent model weights which minimizes both the memory and latency required to serve a model. Often, the goal is to quantize the bit representation while preserving equivalent performance. Quan- tization approaches can be broadly categorized into: 1. Quantization-awaretraining (QAT)(Zafriretal.,2019;Krishnamoorthi,2018)вҖ“Quantization- aware training (QAT) involves pre-training with simulated quantization, enabling parameters to adjust to lower precision grids. This requires estimating the derivative of non-differentiable quantization operators, performing full backpropagation throughout the entire model, and training with the entire training dataset. However, this method can be computationally ex- pensive, particularly for large language models. 2. Quantization-aware finetuning(Yao et al., 2022; Frantar et al., 2022; Zhuo et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021; Hubara et al., 2020; Nagel et al., 2020) (QAF) is a more efficient approach that utilizes a pretrained model and a small subset of training data (i.e., hundreds of samples) to optimize performance under quantization. By simulating quantization and optimizing a small range of parameters at a time, no backpropagation is needed while the quantization loss can be reduced. 3. One-shot post-training quantization (PTQ) (Xiao et al., 2022; Dettmers et al., 2022) unlike QAT and QAF, does not involve optimization. Instead, it directly maps data from a high precision range to a low precision range based on a hand-picked mapping function. 30Given the complexities of successfully training a large language model (Zhang et al., 2022; Rae et al., 2021), post-training quantization (PTQ) methods are extremely attractive as these techniques require the least modification to pretrained parameters. This is the focus of our exploration in this work. C.1 Introduction to Post-Training Integer Quantization Approaches Below section introduces widely used quantization methods and provides context about the differ- ences between these methods. The quantization strategy for weights and activations can be broadly classified into three categories: C.1.1 Weight-only Quantization Weight-only quantization has proven extremely effective in making large language models accessible by enabling inference in a resource-constrained environment while maintaining the FP16 model quality (Gerganov, 2023; Frantar et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2022). Weight-only quantization provides improvements in latency due to a reduction in time taken for parameter fetching from GPU global memory, however, the actual Matrix-Matrix multiplication (GEMM) operations are carried out at higher precision in FP16 - allowing modest gains on platforms without dedicated lower-precision GEMM operations support. C.1.2 Weight and Activation Quantization As large language models are scaled, progressively they become compute-bound and the improve- ments due to weight-only quantization stagnate. However, in this regime, using efficient kernels that leverage specialized lower-precision cores in modern GPUs to directly perform the actual Matrix- Matrix multiplication operation at lower precision enables large latency gains - due to the increased throughput of INT8 tensor cores over FP16 Tensor Cores (Nvidia). As this quantization technique scales the best, this is going to be our focus in this work. To-date quantization ofboth the activations and weights of very large models (>6.7B parameters) has proven challenging - leading to a large drop in performance. C.1.3 Quantization by Mixed-Precision Decomposition In the quantization strategies mentioned above, even though the various weights and activations might be stored in different precisions; all the computations in a single operation are carried out at the same precision (FP16 or INT8). In contrast,LLM.int8() (Dettmers et al., 2022) proposes to decompose the matrix multiplication to compute a small fraction of elements at a higher precision (FP16) while the bulk of the computations is performed at low precision (INT8). This approach has a similar footprint to that of weight-only quantization but practical latency gains are limited or potentially worse. While this approach has theoretical latency benefits due to the bulk of the computation being performed at lower precision, in practice without specialized hardware (Dash et al., 2022; Dash & Mukhopadhyay, 2020), the lack of specialized kernels on GPUs and additional kernel calls required to ready the inputs and weights for mixed-precision computation negates the projected benefits. In this work we focus on exploring optimization choices which mitigate quantiza- 31tion trade-offs for bothweight-only quantizationand the far more challengingweight and activation quantization. 32

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Challenges the notion that severe post-training quantization (PTQ) degradation in large LLMs is an inherent emergent property of scale. Demonstrates that with a quantization-friendly training recipe (higher weight decay, gradient clipping, no dropout, bf16 training), models from 410M to 52B parameters can be INT8-quantized (weights and activations) with negligible performance loss (вүҲ0вҖ“0.3% average; reported вүҲ0.26% at 52B). Provides a controlled large-scale study isolating optimization factors, and offers weight/activation analyses (RMSE, activation/std, LayerNorm gain spread, spectral norms) explaining robustness. Also shows strong robustness under 4-bit weight-only PTQ (3.6% mean drop at 52B) compared to prior models.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Controlled ablation of pre-training optimization choices while holding architecture constant (decoder-only GPT-style, pre-norm). Varied: weight decay {0.001, 0.01, 0.1}, gradient clipping {None, 1}, dropout {0, 0.1, 0.4, 0.8}, half-precision type {bf16, fp16}.
- One-shot PTQ with vector-wise INT8 quantization of both weights and activations: per-row (activations) and per-column (weights) uniform symmetric scaling; INT8 GEMMs followed by dequantization back to FP16. Also tested INT4 column-wise weight-only PTQ.
- Diagnostic analyses linking robustness to distributions: activation RMSE between original/dequantized, per-token activation std, LayerNorm gain parameter std (outlier amplification), and spectral norms of key weight matrices (e.g., attn-kqv-proj). Comparisons against OPT and BLOOM highlight distributional differences associated with quantization sensitivity.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Models: Autoregressive decoder-only Transformers (GPT-style, separate input/output embeddings), max seq length 2048, SentencePiece vocab 51,200. Sizes: 410M, 6B, 13B, 52B.
- Training: Mixed precision (bf16 or fp16; fp32 params), AdamW, batch 256, cosine LR with 1,500 warmup, GeLU, pre-norm. Data: mixture of Common Crawl and C4. Infrastructure: TPU-v4 for training (e.g., 6B: ~72h on 128 cores for 75k steps; 52B: ~20 days on 2,048 TPU cores), A100 GPUs for evaluation; FAX/JAX-based scaling.
- Protocol: Early-stage ablations on 410M/6B (up to 75k steps) to identify robust recipes, then full training of 6B/13B/52B with best recipe (wd=0.1, gc=1, dropout=0, bf16).
- Evaluation: Zero-shot on 8 tasksвҖ”Copa (test/dev), HellaSwag, PIQA (validation), StoryCloze, WinoGrande, Paralex, LAMBADAвҖ”with task-specific metrics (MC accuracy; LAMBADA exact-match; Paralex likelihood). Report average percent degradation normalized per task. Also compared to OPT/BLOOM PTQ results from prior work.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Architectural and objective choices held fixed; does not explore alternative architectures (e.g., attention variants, normalization schemes) or training objectives.
- Evaluation limited to a small zero-shot benchmark suite; no finetuned or broader downstream coverage, and no latency/throughput benchmarks despite discussing kernel benefits.
- Heavy reliance on bf16 training for robustness; availability of bf16 training hardware may limit reproducibility for some users.
- PTQ-only focus; no quantization-aware training/finetuning comparisons; no calibration data studies.
- Outlier-dimension metrics from prior work did not correlate here; generalizable proxy metrics remain open.
- Results on KV-cache quantization are discussed as motivation but not empirically evaluated.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Explore architectural and normalization designs (e.g., modified LayerNorm, residual scaling) and training objectives that further suppress outliers and enable lower-bit activations (INT4/INT3) at scale.
- Develop reliable, generalizable proxy metrics for quantization robustness beyond ad hoc outlier counts; formalize early-checkpoint diagnostics for predicting PTQ sensitivity.
- Extend study to broader tasks (reasoning, multilingual, generative quality, safety) and finetuned settings, with systematic latency/throughput and memory measurements on diverse hardware.
- Integrate quantization-friendly pretraining with quantization-aware training/finetuning to push precision boundaries while preserving accuracy.
- Investigate data curation and scaling laws linking training stability (e.g., bf16 vs fp16 numerics), activation distributions, and quantization outcomes.
- Evaluate end-to-end inference stacks including KV-cache quantization and specialized kernels to realize practical speedups on GPUs/CPUs/accelerators.

research_study_list.title: Overcoming Oscillations in Quantization-Aware Training
research_study_list.abstract:
When training neural networks with simulated quantization, we observe that
quantized weights can, rather unexpectedly, oscillate between two grid-points.
The importance of this effect and its impact on quantization-aware training
(QAT) are not well-understood or investigated in literature. In this paper, we
delve deeper into the phenomenon of weight oscillations and show that it can
lead to a significant accuracy degradation due to wrongly estimated
batch-normalization statistics during inference and increased noise during
training. These effects are particularly pronounced in low-bit ($\leq$ 4-bits)
quantization of efficient networks with depth-wise separable layers, such as
MobileNets and EfficientNets. In our analysis we investigate several previously
proposed QAT algorithms and show that most of these are unable to overcome
oscillations. Finally, we propose two novel QAT algorithms to overcome
oscillations during training: oscillation dampening and iterative weight
freezing. We demonstrate that our algorithms achieve state-of-the-art accuracy
for low-bit (3 & 4 bits) weight and activation quantization of efficient
architectures, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and EfficentNet-lite on
ImageNet. Our source code is available at
{https://github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/oscillations-qat}.
research_study_list.full_text: Overcoming Oscillations in Quantization-Aware Training Markus Nagel* 1 Marios Fournarakis* 1 Yelysei Bondarenko1 Tijmen Blankevoort1 Abstract When training neural networks with simulated quantization, we observe that quantized weights can, rather unexpectedly, oscillate between two grid-points. The importance of this effect and its impact on quantization-aware training (QAT) are not well-understood or investigated in literature. In this paper, we delve deeper into the phenomenon of weight oscillations and show that it can lead to a signiп¬Ғcant accuracy degradation due to wrongly estimated batch-normalization statistics during inference and increased noise during training. These effects are particularly pronounced in low-bit ( вүӨ4-bits) quantization of efп¬Ғcient networks with depth-wise separable layers, such as MobileNets and Efп¬ҒcientNets. In our analysis we investigate several previously proposed QAT algorithms and show that most of these are unable to overcome oscillations. Finally, we propose two novel QAT algorithms to overcome oscillations during training: oscil- lation dampening and iterative weight freezing. We demonstrate that our algorithms achieve state-of-the-art accuracy for low-bit (3 & 4 bits) weight and activation quantization of efп¬Ғcient architectures, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and Efп¬ҒcentNet-lite on ImageNet. Our source code is available at https:// github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/ oscillations-qat. 1. Introduction Quantization is one of the most successful methods for optimizing neural networks for efп¬Ғcient inference and on- device execution while maintaining a high accuracy. By compressing the weights and activations from the regular *Equal contribution 1Qualcomm AI Research, an initiative of Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.. Correspondence to: Markus Nagel <markusn@qti.qualcomm.com>, Marios Fournarakis <mfournar@qti.qualcomm.com>. Proceedings of the39 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy- right 2022 by the author(s). 32-bit п¬Ӯoating-point format to more efп¬Ғcient low bit п¬Ғxed- point representations, such as INT8, we can reduce power consumption and accelerate inference when deploying neu- ral networks on edge devices (Horowitz, 2014). Despite its clear power and latency beneп¬Ғts, quantization comes at the cost of added noise due to the reduced precision. However, researchers in recent years have shown that neural networks are robust to this noise and can be quantized to 8-bits with a minimal drop in accuracy using post-training quantization techniques (PTQ) (Nagel et al., 2019; Banner et al., 2019; Nahshan et al., 2020). PTQ can be very efп¬Ғcient and, generally, only requires access to a small calibration dataset, but suffers when applied to low-bit quantization (вүӨ 4-bits) of neural networks. Meanwhile, quantization-aware training (QAT) has become the de-facto standard method for achieving low-bit quantization while maintaining near full-precision accuracy (Krishnamoorthi, 2018; Esser et al., 2020; Nagel et al., 2021). By simulating the quantization operation during training or п¬Ғne-tuning, the network can adapt to the quantization noise and reach better solutions than with PTQ. In this paper, we focus on the oscillations of quantized weights that occur during quantization-aware training. This is a little-known and under-investigated phenomenon in the optimization of quantized neural networks, with signiп¬Ғ- cant consequences for the network during and after training. When using the popular straight-through estimator(STE) (Bengio et al., 2013) for QAT, weights seemingly randomly oscillate between adjacent quantization levels leading to detrimental noise during the optimization process. Equipped with this insight, we investigate recent advances in QAT that claim improved performance and assess their effectiveness in addressing this oscillatory behavior. An adverse symptom of weight oscillations is that they can corrupt the estimated inference statistics of the batch- normalization layer collected during training, leading to poor validation accuracy. We п¬Ғnd that this effect is partic- ularly pronounced in low-bit quantization of efп¬Ғcient net- works with depth-wise separable layers, such as MobileNets or Efп¬ҒcientNets, but can be addressed effectively by re- estimating the batch-normalization statistics after training. While batch-normalization re-estimation overcomes one sig- niп¬Ғcant symptom of the oscillations, it does not tackle its arXiv:2203.11086v2  [cs.LG]  28 Jun 2022Overcoming Oscillations in QAT root cause. To this end, we propose two novel algorithms that are effective at reducing oscillations: oscillation damp- ening and iterative weight freezing. By addressing oscilla- tions at their source, our methods improve accuracy beyond the level of batch-normalization re-estimation. We show that both methods achieve state-of-the-art results for 4 and 3-bit quantization of efп¬Ғcient networks, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and Efп¬ҒcentNet-lite on ImageNet. 2. Oscillations in QAT We п¬Ғrst investigate why weights oscillate in quantization- aware training and how this phenomenon affects neural network training in practice. 2.1. Quantization-aware training One of the most effective ways to quantize a neural network is by training the network with simulated quantization. Dur- ing the forward pass, п¬Ӯoating-point weights and activation are quantized using the quantization function q(В·). It takes input vector w and returns quantized output ЛҶw given by: ЛҶw = q(w; s,n,p ) = sВ·clip (вҢҠw s вҢү ,n,p ) , (1) where вҢҠВ·вҢүis the round-to-nearest operator, clip (В·,Оұ,ОІ ) is a clipping function with lower and upper bounds Оұand ОІ, respectively, s is a scaling factor, and n and p the lower and upper quantization thresholds. In this formulation, the quantized weights ЛҶw are the ones used during inference, whereas the original п¬Ӯoating-point weights w only serve as proxies for the optimization and are commonly referred to as latent weightsor shadow-weights. A fundamental challenge in the QAT formulation is that the rounding function in equation (1) does not have a meaning- ful gradient, which makes gradient-based training impossi- ble. One of the most popular techniques for alleviating this issue involves using the straight-through estimator (STE) (Bengio et al., 2013; Hinton, 2012) to approximate the true gradient during training. In practice, this means that we ap- proximate the gradient of the rounding operator as 1 within the quantization limits. We can thus deп¬Ғne the gradient of the loss Lwith respect to w as: вҲӮL вҲӮw = вҲӮL вҲӮЛҶw В·1nвүӨw/sвүӨp, (2) where 1 is the indicator function that is 1 if w falls within the quantization grid, and 0 otherwise, such that there is no gradient outside of the representable quantization region. The STE gradient approximation has been widely adopted in recent literature closing the gap between quantized and full- precision accuracy for a wide range of tasks and networks (Gupta et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2016; Krishnamoorthi, 2018; Jain et al., 2019; Esser et al., 2020; Bhalgat et al., 2020; Nagel et al., 2021). 2.2. Oscillations Despite its wide adaptation and enormous success in QAT, the STE has a counter-intuitive and very interesting side- effect. The STE causes implicit stochasticity during the optimization process due to the latent weights oscillating around the decision boundary between adjacent quantized states. This phenomenon was recently also observed by DВҙefossez et al. (2021). To illustrate this, we propose a simple toy regression exam- ple that we will refer to frequently throughout this paper. We start with an optimal п¬Ӯoating-point weight, wвҲ—, as target for our 1D toy regression problem and sample a data vector x from a distribution X with bounded variance such that, E [ x2] = Пғ2, where 0 < Пғ2 <вҲһ. Then we optimize the least-squares problem min w L(w) = ExвҲјX [1 2 (xwвҲ—вҲ’xq(w))2 ] , (3) where q(В·) is the quantizer from equation (1) and wis the latent weight. We optimize the objective using the STE for- mulation for the gradients. From п¬Ғgure 1 (left), we can see that as the latent weight wapproaches the optimal value wвҲ—, it starts oscillating around the decision threshold between the quantization levels above wвҶ‘and below wвҶ“the optimal value, as opposed to converging to the region closer to the optimal value q(wвҲ—). The weight oscillates around the decision threshold due to the fact that the gradient in equation(2) is constant above the threshold, pushing the latent weight down towards wвҶ“, and constant below the threshold pushing the latent weight up towards wвҶ‘. Please refer to appendix A.1 for an analytical expression of this gradient. The induced oscillations happen irrespective of the learning rate. In appendix A.3 we show that decreasing the learning rate reduces the amplitude of the oscillations but do not affect their frequency. The frequency of the oscillation is depends on the distance of the optimal value from its closest quantization level, d= |wвҲ—вҲ’q(wвҲ—)|. LetвҖҷs assume that q(wвҲ—) = wвҶ‘, then the gradient below the threshold isk= s/dвҲ’1 times larger than the gradient above, where sis scaling factor or quantization step-size and s>d . Interpreting the gradient as the velocity of the latent weight, wneeds to travel for kiterations before it crosses the threshold and its velocity/gradient is reversed. In appendix A.2 we show experimentally that the oscillation frequency is indeed directly proportional to closeness of wвҲ— to q(wвҲ—). It is interesting to note that this behavior bears similarities to stochastic rounding (Gupta et al., 2015), where the close- ness of the latent weight to the quantization level is related to the probability of rounding to that level. However, in STE the source of the stochasticity stems from the discrete natureOvercoming Oscillations in QAT Figure 1.Oscillation of a single weight in toy-regression problem for various gradient estimator methods: STE (left), EWGS (J. Lee, 2021) (middle), and DSQ (Gong et al., 2019) (right). Figure 2.Progression of weights in the п¬Ғrst channel of the п¬Ғrst depth-wise separable layer (conv.1.0) during the last 1000 itera- tions of training MobileNetV2 on ImageNet. The top plot shows the integer weights and the bottom plot zooms in on the decision boundary between 0 and 1 for the corresponding latent weights. of the gradients and not from sampling. We note that oscil- lations are not unique to the vanilla STE, but are present in several variations of the STE proposed in literature, a small subset of which we present in п¬Ғgure 1. 2.3. Oscillations in practice These oscillations are not just an unfortunate side-effect of this п¬Ғctitious toy example. They are indeed present in larger neural networks with signiп¬Ғcant implications for their opti- mization. Figure 2 shows the progression of 3-bit quantized weights in a depth-wise separable layer of MobileNetV2 close to convergence, trained using LSQ (Esser et al., 2020) on ImageNet. We observe that many of the weights appear to randomly oscillate between two adjacent quantization levels. In п¬Ғgure 3, we also see that after the supposed convergence of the network, a large fraction of the latent weights lie right at the decision boundary between grid points. This further reinforces the observation that a signiп¬Ғcant proportion of weights oscillate and do not converge. We identify two major issues associated with the oscillations in neural network training: Wrong estimation of the batch- Figure 3.Weight distribution in depth-wise separable layer of third inverted residual block of MobileNetV2 (conv.3.1). Histogram of latent weights (left). Histogram of the distance of latent weight from their closest quantization grid-point (wint вҲ’w/s) (right). normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) inference statistics, and an adverse effect on the optimization of the network. 2.3.1. T HE EFFECT ON BATCH -NORMALIZATION During training batch-normalization layers track the expo- nential moving average (EMA) of the mean and variance for each layerвҖҷs output, so that it can be used during inference as an approximation of the real sample statistics. When training full-precision neural networks we can expect that weights will change very slowly close to convergence. As a result, the output statistics of each layer are expected to be quite stable across iterations and therefore the EMA is a good estimate of the statistics. However, oscillations in QAT can lead to rapid changes of the integer weights (see in п¬Ғgure 2) leading to signiп¬Ғcant distribution shift between iterations, even close to convergence. The sudden and large changes in the output distribution induced by oscillations can corrupt the EMA statistics leading to a signiп¬Ғcant degra- dation in accuracy. In fact, there are two factors that amplify this effect: the weight bit-width and the number of weights per output channel. The lower the bit-width b, the larger the distance becomes between quantization levels, as it is proportional to1/2b. As the oscillating weights move from one quantization level to the other, they cause a proportionally larger shift in the out- put distribution. The second important factor is the numberOvercoming Oscillations in QAT Network Layer max DKL E[DKL] ResNet18 layer1.0.conv1 0.0059 0.0002 ResNet18 layer1.0.conv2 0.0130 0.0014 ResNet18 layer3.0.conv1 0.0006 0.0001 MobileNetV2 conv3.0 (PW) 0.7858 0.0292 MobileNetV2 conv3.1 (DW) 55.3782 1.25464 MobileNetV2 conv3.2 (PW) 0.0065 0.0012 MobileNetV2 conv10.0 (PW) 0.0037 0.0004 MobileNetV2 conv10.1 (DW) 27.2618 0.2900 MobileNetV2 conv10.2 (PW) 0.0267 0.0034 Table 1.Discrepancy between estimated and actual population statistics of batch-normalization following convolutional layers in ResNet18 and MobileNetV2 with weights quantized to 3 bits. We report the maximum and mean KL-divergence across the output channels of each layer. DS: depth-wise separable, PW: point-wise. of weights per output channel. The smaller the number of weights, the larger the contribution of individual weights to the п¬Ғnal accumulation. When the number of accumulations increases, the effects of oscillations average out due to the law of large numbers. In table 1, we use the KL-divergence1 to quantify the dis- crepancy between population and estimated statistics. We indeed observe that KL divergence is much larger for depth- wise separable layers than in point-wise convolutions in MobileNetV2 and the full convolutions in ResNet18. A cheap and straightforward solution to this problem is to re-estimate the batch-normalization statistics with a small subset of data after training. This method, which we call batch-normalization (BN) re-estimation, is sometimes used in stochastic quantization formulations (Peters & Welling, 2018; Louizos et al., 2019). However, we argue that it is also essential in deterministic QAT formulations due to the oscillating weights. In table 2, we see that BN re-estimation not only improves the п¬Ғnal quantized accuracy for MobileNetV2 but also re- duces the variance among different seeds. We further ob- serve that the gap in accuracy widens as the bit-width goes down for MobileNetV2, which is not the case for ResNet18. 2.3.2. T HE EFFECT ON TRAINING Next to harming the BN statistics, oscillations can also have a negative effect on the training process itself. To illustrate this we п¬Ғrst show that a converged MobileNetV2 with 3-bit weights can achieve a lower training loss (and higher valida- 1We we assume that population p and estimated q outputs are normally distributed, such that DKL(p,q) = log Пғ2 2 Пғ2 1 + Пғ2 1+(Вө1вҲ’Вө2)2 2Пғ2 2 вҲ’1 2 , where p(x) = N(Вө1,Пғ1) and q(x) =N(Вө2,Пғ2). We report the average of 3 seeds and the STD in the superscript. Method Train Loss Val. Acc. (%) Baseline 1.3566 69.50 SR (mean + std) 1.35470.0053 69.580.09 SR (best) 1.3391 69.85 AdaRound 1.3070 70.12 Freezing - 70.33 Table 3.Ablation study on the effect of oscillations on QAT. Train- ing loss and validation accuracy (%) for MobileNetV2 with 3- bit weight quantization. SR is stochastic rounding of oscillat- ing weights and AdaRound the binary optimization of oscillating weights. tion accuracy) if we randomly sample oscillating weights between their two oscillating states. To this end, we sample all weights that were oscillating at the end of training with a probability proportional to the time spent at each quantized state, i.e. p(wвҶ‘) = Et[wt = wвҶ‘]. We calculate the expec- tation using a exponential moving average over the integer weights as done in line 15 of algorithm 1. In table 3, we present the results of this experiment. We ob- serve that the mean training loss over the sampled networks is similar to that of the п¬Ғnal converged model. However, many samples achieve a lower training loss and the best ran- domly sampled network shows a signiп¬Ғcantly lower training loss. We also perform a binary optimization of the oscil- lating weights using an adaptation of AdaRound (Nagel et al., 2020). We optimize the rounding of all layers si- multaneously on the п¬Ғnal task loss, akin to what is done in the literature with simulated annealing to solve binary optimization problems (Kirkpatrick et al., 1983). We see that this binary optimization signiп¬Ғcantly improves over the best stochastic sample and the original converged network. This suggests that weight oscillations prevent the network from converging to the best local minimum during training, and can be detrimental to the optimization process. Finally, we show that preventing oscillations earlier in train- ing using our oscillation freezing technique (cf. section 4.3) leads to an even higher validation accuracy than the binaryOvercoming Oscillations in QAT optimization of the oscillating weights. This indicates that the oscillations not only prevent QAT from converging to the best local minimum at the end of training, but can also lead the optimizer towards sub-optimal directions earlier in training. 3. Related work Earlier work in QAT (Cai et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2019) investigated different variations of the STE and demonstrate experimentally that the derivative of the clipped ReLU is a better approximation than the vanilla STE (derivative of identity). In practice, the former is the same as setting the gradient outside the quantization grid to zero, as per equation (2), and is the most widely used formulation in QAT literature (Choi et al., 2018; Esser et al., 2020; Uhlich et al., 2020). Despite the success of the STE trick in practice, a lot of prior work has focused on the non-optimality of the STE as a gradient approximator. DВҙefossez et al. (2021) claim that the STE causes instability during training due to weights oscillating leading to biased gradients and weights. For this reason, they propose replacing the rounding operation with additive Gaussian noise in the forward pass to sim- ulate quantization noise. Whereas weight oscillations are indeed an important side-effect of the STE, the idea that the STE yields biased gradients in a deterministic training setting is opaque, as we discuss in more detail in appendix A.4. Nguyen et al. (2020) use a toy example to claim that solutions under STE can lead to a sub-optimal quantized solution but do not recognize that the quantized weight will oscillate around the optimum rather than converge to the wrong value. Based on their observation, they propose an absolute-cosine regularization for the full-precision weights to make them more quantization-friendly but do not com- pare to other competing QAT methods. Other work focuses on alternative gradient estimatorsfor back-propagation. Despite seeming differences in their mo- tivation and formulations, all these methods in on way or another propose adaptations of STE that boil down into two main categories: multiplicative or additive. Multiplica- tive methods apply a scaling to the data-gradient, whereas additive methods add an input-independent term to the gra- dient through regularization. We provide more details and derivations in appendix A.1. Multiplicative J. Lee (2021) (EWGS) acknowledge that the STE generates coarse gradients that treat all latent weights within a quantization bin equally regardless of their distance from its centre and propose scaling the gradient by this distance. However, they do not provide any evidence on why and how these coarse gradient harm optimization. Kim et al. (2020) arrive to a similar formulation but are motivated by robust quantization. They claim that clustering latent weights around bin centres makes them more robust when quantized to different bit widths. In both these methods, the gradient scales linearly with the distance of the latent weight from its quantized value. Gong et al. (2019) (DSQ) try to alleviate the gradient error that the STE causes during low-bit quantization but do not provide any evidence about the source or magnitude of this error. They propose using a series of hyperbolic tangent functions to simulate the rounding function in the backward pass. This leads to a non-linear scaling of the STE gradient, with the gradient increasing with the distance from the bin centre. Yang et al. (2019) also approximate the rounding function with a stack of sigmoid functions to address the gradient mismatchproblem. They parameterize the sigmoid with a temperature, which increases during training to better approximate the step function. As we show in the appendix, these multiplicative methods can affect the magnitude of the oscillations but cannot stop them from happening. In п¬Ғgure 1, we visualize the effect of EWGS and DSQ gradients on the 1D regression problem, described in section 2.2. It is evident, that neither of these methods overcome oscillations. Additive Various regularizations have been proposed in literature to make the weight distribution more quantization- friendly. Chai (2021) apply an L2 regularization on the difference between quantized and п¬Ӯoating-point weights, while Han et al. (2021) apply bin regularization to enforce the weights to cluster at the quantization grid points. Their bin regularization method is similar to our proposed os- cillation dampeningmethod described later in section 4.2. However, their motivation is to form sharper weight distri- bution around the bin centre that reduce the quantization error rather than preventing oscillations. In contrast to the multiplicative methods, these additive methods add a term to the gradient and can thus dampen oscillations by pulling the latent weights towards the grid points. Park & Yoo (2020) recognize that the STE can cause acti- vation instability during training of low-bit quantized Mo- bileNets and propose progressively freezing whole layers during training. Chen et al. (2019) approximate the gradient using a small neural network, whose parameters are learnt using meta-learning but is limited to binary weights. 4. Overcoming oscillations in QAT Now that we have established that oscillations can have a negative effect during optimization, especially for low-bit quantization, we focus on how to overcome them. First we introduce a metric for quantifying oscillations and then we propose two novel techniques aimed at preventing oscilla- tions at their source during quantization-aware training.Overcoming Oscillations in QAT 4.1. Quantifying Oscillations Before we can address the oscillations, we need a way of detecting and measuring them during training. We propose calculating the frequency of oscillations over time using an exponential moving average (EMA). We can then deп¬Ғne a minimum frequency as a threshold for oscillating weights. For an oscillation to occur in iteration tit needs two satisfy two conditions: 1. The integer value of the weight needs to change, thus wt int Мё= wtвҲ’1 int where wt int = clip (вҢҠw s вҢү ,n,p ) is the integer value of the quantized weight. 2. The direction of the change in the integer domain needs to be the opposite than that of its previous change, thus ot = sign(вҲҶt int) Мё= sign(вҲҶП„ int) where П„ is the iteration of the last change in integer domain and вҲҶt int = wt int вҲ’ wtвҲ’1 int , the direction of the change. We then track the frequency of oscillations over time using an exponential moving average (EMA): ft = mВ·ot + (1 вҲ’m) В·ftвҲ’1. (4) 4.2. Oscillation dampening When weights oscillate, they always move around the deci- sion threshold between two quantization bins. This means that oscillating weights are always close to the edge of the quantization bin. In order to dampen the oscillatory behav- ior, we employ a regularization term that encourages latent weights to be close to the center of the bin rather than its edge. We deп¬Ғne our dampening loss similar to weight decay as: Ldampen = вҲҘЛҶw вҲ’clip (w,sn,sp )вҲҘ2 F, (5) where s, nand pare the quantization parameters as deп¬Ғned in equation (1) and ЛҶw are the bin centers. Note, as the bin centers are our optimization target, no gradients propagate back through this term. Our п¬Ғnal training objective is now: L= Ltask + О»Ldampen. We choose to apply our bin regular- ization in the latent weight domain such that the resulting gradient вҲӮLdampen вҲӮw = 2 (w вҲ’ЛҶw) В·1snвүӨwвүӨsp (6) is independent of the scale sand, therefore, indirectly inde- pendent of the bit-width2. We further clip our latent weights to the range of the quantization grid, such that only weights that do not get clipped during quantization receive a reg- ularization effect. This is important to avoid any harmful 2A similar loss deп¬Ғned in integer domain ( вҲҘwint вҲ’w/sвҲҘ2 F) would lead to a scaling of gradient with 1/s2 and make the regu- larization on w dependent on the scale and bit-width. A drawback of such a regularization is that it does not only affect weights that oscillate but can also hinder the movement of weights that are not in an oscillating state. 4.3. Iterative freezing of oscillating weights We propose another, more targeted, approach to prevent weights from oscillating by freezing them during training. In this method, we track the oscillations frequency per weight during training, as described in equation (4). If the oscilla- tion frequency of any weight exceeds a threshold fth, that weight gets frozen until the end of training. We apply the freezing in the integer domain, such that potential change in the scale sduring optimization does not lead to a different rounding. When a weight oscillates, it does not necessarily spend an equal amount of time at both oscillating states. As we show in the toy example in section 2.2, the likelihood of a weight being in each state linearly depends on distance of that quantized state from the optimal value. As a result, the expectation of all quantized values over time will correspond to the optimal value. Once the frequency of a weight exceeds the threshold, it could be in either of the two quantized states. To freeze the weight to its more frequent state we keep a record of the previous integer values using an exponential moving average (EMA). We then assign the most frequent integer state to the frozen weight by rounding the EMA. We summarize our proposed iterative weight freezing in al- gorithm 1. Note, this algorithm can be used in combination with any gradient-based optimizer and is not limited to aOvercoming Oscillations in QAT particular quantization formulation or gradient estimator. The idea of freezing weights on an iteration level is closely related to iterative pruning (Zhu & Gupta, 2018), where small weights are iteratively pruned (frozen to zero). 5. Experiments In this section, we evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed methods for overcoming oscillations and compare them against other QAT methods on ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015). We focus on low-bit quantization, 4 and 3 bits, of efп¬Ғcient networks with depth-wise separable con- volutions, where the effect of weight oscillation is most pronounced. We start with our ablation studies, where we only quantize the weights in a network and, п¬Ғnally, present results for low-bit weight and activation quantization. 5.1. Experimental setup Quantization We follow the example of existing QAT literature and apply LSQ-type (Esser et al., 2020) weight and activation quantization: we quantize all weights to a low bit-width while keeping the weights of the п¬Ғrst and last layer to 8-bits, and quantize the input to all layers expect for the normalizing layers. We use per-tensor quantization (Krishnamoorthi, 2018) and learn the quantization scaling factor. Optimization In all cases, we start from a pre-trained full- precision network and instantiate the weight and activation quantization parameters using MSE range estimation (Nagel et al., 2021). We use SGD with a momentum of 0.9 and train using a cosine annealing learning-rate decay. We train for 20 epochs with only weight quantization for the ablation studies. For weight and activation quantization in section 5.3, we train all models for 90 epochs. Depending on the network and quantization bit-width we train with a learning rate of either 0.01 or 0.0033. 5.2. Comparison to other QAT methods We compare our methods for overcoming oscillation to other QAT alternatives and demonstrate their effectiveness in low- bit quantization of popular efп¬Ғcient neural networks. To compare to existing methods in the literature we quantize both weights and activations. In table 6, we show the results for MobileNetV2 and demonstrate that both our algorithms outperform all competing QAT techniques in literature for 3 and 4-bit quantization. We also achieve state-of-the-art re- sults for MobileNetV3-Small and Efп¬ҒcentNet-lite in tables 7 and 8, respectively. In all cases, our oscillation prevention methods signiп¬Ғcantly improve (>1%) over the commonly used LSQ baseline, showing the general applicability of our methods to other efп¬Ғcient networks. We note that oscillation dampening leads to an increase ofOvercoming Oscillations in QAT approximately 33% in training time compared to the LSQ baseline. A similar trend was also observed by (Han et al., 2021). On the other hand, iterative weight freezing has a negligible computational overhead while achieving similar performance. 6. Conclusion In this work, we showed that weight oscillations induced by the straight-through estimator and its variations proposed in literature can adversely affect the performance of quan- tized networks when trained with quantization-aware train- ing. The effect of weight oscillations is more pronounced in low-bit quantization of light-weight networks, such as MobileNetV2 that include depth-wise layers with only a few weights per output channel. We demonstrate that these os- cillations not only corrupt the running batch-normalization statistics used during inference but also harm the optimiza- tion process itself, leading to sub-optimal solutions. We propose two methods to tackle this problem: oscillations dampening and iterative weight freezing, and show that both methods lead to state-of-the-art accuracy on ImageNet classiп¬Ғcation for a range of efп¬Ғcient models quantized to low-bits. However, in the earlier stages of training, where the weights move more freely, the dynamics of the oscillations might be more complicated, as the gradient or velocity of the weight depends on the distance from the closest quantization bin. A.4. The bias of the STE In the original paper on the straight-through estimator, Bengio et al. (2013) note that вҖң... (the STE) is clearly a biased estimatorвҖқ. Several authors, such as Chmiel et al. (2022); Fan et al. (2021), have repeated this statement and have argued that this bias is a fundamental drawback of STE when applied to quantization, without deп¬Ғning what this actually means in the context of quantization-aware training. The bias of the STE in the original formulation occurs in a probabilistic setting. LetвҖҷs assume we want to optimize a loss L= Ep(z|Оё) [f(z)] where zis latent parameter coming for a discrete distribution p(z|Оё). To learn the parameter Оёwe needOvercoming Oscillations in QAT to calculate the derivative вҲҮОёEp(z|Оё) [f(z)]. However, due to the discrete nature of pz this derivative does not exist. Instead the STE provides us with the convenient approximation Ep(z|Оё) [вҲҮzf(z)вҲҮОёЛңz], where Лңzis the unquantized version of z. In this case, indeed the STE is in most cases a biased estimator (Pervez et al., 2020), as the expectation of the gradient does not match with the gradient of the expectation. However, it is unclear what the bias of the estimator means in the QAT setting. Quantized networks do not have an inherent probabilistic property, unless explicitly deп¬Ғned so, e.g. in Bayesian neural networks or when training with stochastic rounding (Gupta et al., 2015; Louizos et al., 2019). The gradients for the п¬Ӯoating-point shadow weights are indeed wrong or biased, but these weights are by themselves meaningless because they are not used in the forward pass. As described in (Helwegen et al., 2019) the underlying weights of a neural network when trained with STE are not representative for the networkвҖҷs performance, but rather can be interpreted as a reservoir that slowly accumulates small gradient updates. Therefore, we believe that comparing to п¬Ӯoating-point gradient in the conventional QAT setting can be misleading and nonconstructive.

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Identifies and analyzes a little-known issue in quantization-aware training (QAT): oscillations of quantized weights between adjacent grid points caused by STE-like estimators. Shows these oscillations (i) corrupt batch-normalization (BN) running statisticsвҖ”especially in low-bit (вүӨ4) and depthwise-separable layersвҖ”and (ii) inject optimization noise that prevents convergence to better minima. Demonstrates that many existing QAT methods (e.g., DSQ, EWGS) do not resolve oscillations. Proposes two new algorithmsвҖ”oscillation dampening (bin-center regularization) and iterative weight freezing (EMA-based detection and freezing in integer domain)вҖ”that reduce oscillations and deliver state-of-the-art low-bit accuracy on ImageNet for MobileNetV2/V3 and EfficientNet-lite. Provides practical guidance that BN re-estimation post-training is essential and introduces a metric to quantify oscillations.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Phenomenon analysis: 1D toy regression with analytical gradient updates to explain oscillation dynamics under STE and variants (multiplicative vs additive gradient modifications). Empirical visualization of oscillations in MobileNetV2 layers and distributional shift near bin boundaries.
- Oscillation metric: Per-weight exponential moving average (EMA) of oscillation frequency based on changes in integer quantized values and direction flips.
- BN re-estimation: Post-training re-computation of BN running mean/variance with calibration data to fix corrupted inference statistics.
- Oscillation dampening: Additive regularization that pulls latent weights toward quantization bin centers: L_dampen = ||Еө вҲ’ clip(w, sn, sp)||^2_F, applied within quantization range; cosine-annealed О» to avoid early over-regularization.
- Iterative weight freezing: Detect high-frequency oscillators (ft > fth) and freeze their integer values to the most frequent state via EMA of integer weights; thresholds annealed with a cosine schedule. Freezing applied in integer domain to be robust to scale updates.
- Quantization/training setup: LSQ-style learned scales; per-tensor quantization; 8-bit for first/last layers; activations quantized except normalization layers; SGD with momentum and cosine LR; initialization of scales via MSE range estimation.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Dataset/Task: ImageNet classification (top-1 accuracy reported).
- Models: MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3-Small, EfficientNet-lite; emphasis on depthwise-separable layers where oscillations are pronounced.
- Bit-widths: Weights and activations at 4 and 3 bits; first/last layers at 8 bits. Ablations with weights-only quantization.
- Baselines/Comparisons: LSQ, PACT, DSQ, EWGS, Bin Regularization (BR). Also stochastic rounding sampling and AdaRound-based binary optimization on final models to assess optimization effects.
- Training details: Start from pretrained FP32; LSQ-type QAT with learned scales; per-tensor quantization; SGD momentum 0.9; cosine LR; LR вҲҲ {0.01, 0.003}; weights-only ablations trained 20 epochs; full W/A trained 90 epochs; some results averaged over 3 seeds.
- Validation methods: Pre-BN vs post-BN re-estimation accuracy; KL divergence between estimated and true BN output statistics to quantify mismatch (large in depthwise layers, small in pointwise/full conv).
- Key results: MobileNetV2 4/4: LSQ 69.5 вҶ’ 70.5/70.6 (dampen/freeze); 3/3: LSQ 65.3 вҶ’ 67.8/67.6. MobileNetV3-Small 4/4: LSQ 61.0 вҶ’ 63.7; 3/3: 52.0 вҶ’ 59.0. EfficientNet-lite 4/4: 72.3 вҶ’ 73.5; 3/3: 69.7 вҶ’ 71.1. BN re-estimation notably boosts accuracy for low-bit MobileNetV2 (e.g., 3-bit pre-BN 64.97 вҶ’ post-BN 69.50).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Focused on image classification (ImageNet) and a subset of efficient CNNs; generalization to other tasks (detection, segmentation), architectures (transformers), or datasets is not evaluated.
- Techniques primarily target weight oscillations; activation oscillations and alternative normalizations are not deeply explored.
- Per-tensor quantization used; per-channel or more advanced schemes not evaluated.
- Excessive dampening or overly aggressive freezing early in training can harm accuracy; schedules require tuning.
- Oscillation dampening increases training time by ~33%; iterative freezing has negligible overhead but still adds bookkeeping complexity.
- Reliance on BN re-estimation post-training; calibration data requirements and size are not quantified.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend and validate oscillation mitigation to other architectures (e.g., transformers), tasks (detection/segmentation), and datasets.
- Investigate activation oscillations and interactions with different normalization schemes (LayerNorm/GroupNorm) or BN folding.
- Explore per-channel and mixed-precision quantization combined with oscillation control and automatic policy/search for schedules (О», fth).
- Provide theoretical convergence/stability guarantees for dampening/freezing under STE-like estimators.
- Integrate with advanced PTQ/QAT hybrids and alternative gradient estimators; study interplay with stochastic rounding and adaptive rounding during training.
- Hardware-aware evaluation: measure latency/energy gains, memory overhead of tracking metrics, and deployment robustness.
- Automated calibration for BN re-estimation and principled selection of calibration set size; investigate on-the-fly statistics correction during training.

research_study_list.title: Overcoming Oscillations in Quantization-Aware Training
research_study_list.abstract:
When training neural networks with simulated quantization, we observe that
quantized weights can, rather unexpectedly, oscillate between two grid-points.
The importance of this effect and its impact on quantization-aware training
(QAT) are not well-understood or investigated in literature. In this paper, we
delve deeper into the phenomenon of weight oscillations and show that it can
lead to a significant accuracy degradation due to wrongly estimated
batch-normalization statistics during inference and increased noise during
training. These effects are particularly pronounced in low-bit ($\leq$ 4-bits)
quantization of efficient networks with depth-wise separable layers, such as
MobileNets and EfficientNets. In our analysis we investigate several previously
proposed QAT algorithms and show that most of these are unable to overcome
oscillations. Finally, we propose two novel QAT algorithms to overcome
oscillations during training: oscillation dampening and iterative weight
freezing. We demonstrate that our algorithms achieve state-of-the-art accuracy
for low-bit (3 & 4 bits) weight and activation quantization of efficient
architectures, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and EfficentNet-lite on
ImageNet. Our source code is available at
{https://github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/oscillations-qat}.
research_study_list.full_text: Overcoming Oscillations in Quantization-Aware Training Markus Nagel* 1 Marios Fournarakis* 1 Yelysei Bondarenko1 Tijmen Blankevoort1 Abstract When training neural networks with simulated quantization, we observe that quantized weights can, rather unexpectedly, oscillate between two grid-points. The importance of this effect and its impact on quantization-aware training (QAT) are not well-understood or investigated in literature. In this paper, we delve deeper into the phenomenon of weight oscillations and show that it can lead to a signiп¬Ғcant accuracy degradation due to wrongly estimated batch-normalization statistics during inference and increased noise during training. These effects are particularly pronounced in low-bit ( вүӨ4-bits) quantization of efп¬Ғcient networks with depth-wise separable layers, such as MobileNets and Efп¬ҒcientNets. In our analysis we investigate several previously proposed QAT algorithms and show that most of these are unable to overcome oscillations. Finally, we propose two novel QAT algorithms to overcome oscillations during training: oscil- lation dampening and iterative weight freezing. We demonstrate that our algorithms achieve state-of-the-art accuracy for low-bit (3 & 4 bits) weight and activation quantization of efп¬Ғcient architectures, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and Efп¬ҒcentNet-lite on ImageNet. Our source code is available at https:// github.com/qualcomm-ai-research/ oscillations-qat. 1. Introduction Quantization is one of the most successful methods for optimizing neural networks for efп¬Ғcient inference and on- device execution while maintaining a high accuracy. By compressing the weights and activations from the regular *Equal contribution 1Qualcomm AI Research, an initiative of Qualcomm Technologies, Inc.. Correspondence to: Markus Nagel <markusn@qti.qualcomm.com>, Marios Fournarakis <mfournar@qti.qualcomm.com>. Proceedings of the39 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy- right 2022 by the author(s). 32-bit п¬Ӯoating-point format to more efп¬Ғcient low bit п¬Ғxed- point representations, such as INT8, we can reduce power consumption and accelerate inference when deploying neu- ral networks on edge devices (Horowitz, 2014). Despite its clear power and latency beneп¬Ғts, quantization comes at the cost of added noise due to the reduced precision. However, researchers in recent years have shown that neural networks are robust to this noise and can be quantized to 8-bits with a minimal drop in accuracy using post-training quantization techniques (PTQ) (Nagel et al., 2019; Banner et al., 2019; Nahshan et al., 2020). PTQ can be very efп¬Ғcient and, generally, only requires access to a small calibration dataset, but suffers when applied to low-bit quantization (вүӨ 4-bits) of neural networks. Meanwhile, quantization-aware training (QAT) has become the de-facto standard method for achieving low-bit quantization while maintaining near full-precision accuracy (Krishnamoorthi, 2018; Esser et al., 2020; Nagel et al., 2021). By simulating the quantization operation during training or п¬Ғne-tuning, the network can adapt to the quantization noise and reach better solutions than with PTQ. In this paper, we focus on the oscillations of quantized weights that occur during quantization-aware training. This is a little-known and under-investigated phenomenon in the optimization of quantized neural networks, with signiп¬Ғ- cant consequences for the network during and after training. When using the popular straight-through estimator(STE) (Bengio et al., 2013) for QAT, weights seemingly randomly oscillate between adjacent quantization levels leading to detrimental noise during the optimization process. Equipped with this insight, we investigate recent advances in QAT that claim improved performance and assess their effectiveness in addressing this oscillatory behavior. An adverse symptom of weight oscillations is that they can corrupt the estimated inference statistics of the batch- normalization layer collected during training, leading to poor validation accuracy. We п¬Ғnd that this effect is partic- ularly pronounced in low-bit quantization of efп¬Ғcient net- works with depth-wise separable layers, such as MobileNets or Efп¬ҒcientNets, but can be addressed effectively by re- estimating the batch-normalization statistics after training. While batch-normalization re-estimation overcomes one sig- niп¬Ғcant symptom of the oscillations, it does not tackle its arXiv:2203.11086v2  [cs.LG]  28 Jun 2022Overcoming Oscillations in QAT root cause. To this end, we propose two novel algorithms that are effective at reducing oscillations: oscillation damp- ening and iterative weight freezing. By addressing oscilla- tions at their source, our methods improve accuracy beyond the level of batch-normalization re-estimation. We show that both methods achieve state-of-the-art results for 4 and 3-bit quantization of efп¬Ғcient networks, such as MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and Efп¬ҒcentNet-lite on ImageNet. 2. Oscillations in QAT We п¬Ғrst investigate why weights oscillate in quantization- aware training and how this phenomenon affects neural network training in practice. 2.1. Quantization-aware training One of the most effective ways to quantize a neural network is by training the network with simulated quantization. Dur- ing the forward pass, п¬Ӯoating-point weights and activation are quantized using the quantization function q(В·). It takes input vector w and returns quantized output ЛҶw given by: ЛҶw = q(w; s,n,p ) = sВ·clip (вҢҠw s вҢү ,n,p ) , (1) where вҢҠВ·вҢүis the round-to-nearest operator, clip (В·,Оұ,ОІ ) is a clipping function with lower and upper bounds Оұand ОІ, respectively, s is a scaling factor, and n and p the lower and upper quantization thresholds. In this formulation, the quantized weights ЛҶw are the ones used during inference, whereas the original п¬Ӯoating-point weights w only serve as proxies for the optimization and are commonly referred to as latent weightsor shadow-weights. A fundamental challenge in the QAT formulation is that the rounding function in equation (1) does not have a meaning- ful gradient, which makes gradient-based training impossi- ble. One of the most popular techniques for alleviating this issue involves using the straight-through estimator (STE) (Bengio et al., 2013; Hinton, 2012) to approximate the true gradient during training. In practice, this means that we ap- proximate the gradient of the rounding operator as 1 within the quantization limits. We can thus deп¬Ғne the gradient of the loss Lwith respect to w as: вҲӮL вҲӮw = вҲӮL вҲӮЛҶw В·1nвүӨw/sвүӨp, (2) where 1 is the indicator function that is 1 if w falls within the quantization grid, and 0 otherwise, such that there is no gradient outside of the representable quantization region. The STE gradient approximation has been widely adopted in recent literature closing the gap between quantized and full- precision accuracy for a wide range of tasks and networks (Gupta et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2016; Krishnamoorthi, 2018; Jain et al., 2019; Esser et al., 2020; Bhalgat et al., 2020; Nagel et al., 2021). 2.2. Oscillations Despite its wide adaptation and enormous success in QAT, the STE has a counter-intuitive and very interesting side- effect. The STE causes implicit stochasticity during the optimization process due to the latent weights oscillating around the decision boundary between adjacent quantized states. This phenomenon was recently also observed by DВҙefossez et al. (2021). To illustrate this, we propose a simple toy regression exam- ple that we will refer to frequently throughout this paper. We start with an optimal п¬Ӯoating-point weight, wвҲ—, as target for our 1D toy regression problem and sample a data vector x from a distribution X with bounded variance such that, E [ x2] = Пғ2, where 0 < Пғ2 <вҲһ. Then we optimize the least-squares problem min w L(w) = ExвҲјX [1 2 (xwвҲ—вҲ’xq(w))2 ] , (3) where q(В·) is the quantizer from equation (1) and wis the latent weight. We optimize the objective using the STE for- mulation for the gradients. From п¬Ғgure 1 (left), we can see that as the latent weight wapproaches the optimal value wвҲ—, it starts oscillating around the decision threshold between the quantization levels above wвҶ‘and below wвҶ“the optimal value, as opposed to converging to the region closer to the optimal value q(wвҲ—). The weight oscillates around the decision threshold due to the fact that the gradient in equation(2) is constant above the threshold, pushing the latent weight down towards wвҶ“, and constant below the threshold pushing the latent weight up towards wвҶ‘. Please refer to appendix A.1 for an analytical expression of this gradient. The induced oscillations happen irrespective of the learning rate. In appendix A.3 we show that decreasing the learning rate reduces the amplitude of the oscillations but do not affect their frequency. The frequency of the oscillation is depends on the distance of the optimal value from its closest quantization level, d= |wвҲ—вҲ’q(wвҲ—)|. LetвҖҷs assume that q(wвҲ—) = wвҶ‘, then the gradient below the threshold isk= s/dвҲ’1 times larger than the gradient above, where sis scaling factor or quantization step-size and s>d . Interpreting the gradient as the velocity of the latent weight, wneeds to travel for kiterations before it crosses the threshold and its velocity/gradient is reversed. In appendix A.2 we show experimentally that the oscillation frequency is indeed directly proportional to closeness of wвҲ— to q(wвҲ—). It is interesting to note that this behavior bears similarities to stochastic rounding (Gupta et al., 2015), where the close- ness of the latent weight to the quantization level is related to the probability of rounding to that level. However, in STE the source of the stochasticity stems from the discrete natureOvercoming Oscillations in QAT Figure 1.Oscillation of a single weight in toy-regression problem for various gradient estimator methods: STE (left), EWGS (J. Lee, 2021) (middle), and DSQ (Gong et al., 2019) (right). Figure 2.Progression of weights in the п¬Ғrst channel of the п¬Ғrst depth-wise separable layer (conv.1.0) during the last 1000 itera- tions of training MobileNetV2 on ImageNet. The top plot shows the integer weights and the bottom plot zooms in on the decision boundary between 0 and 1 for the corresponding latent weights. of the gradients and not from sampling. We note that oscil- lations are not unique to the vanilla STE, but are present in several variations of the STE proposed in literature, a small subset of which we present in п¬Ғgure 1. 2.3. Oscillations in practice These oscillations are not just an unfortunate side-effect of this п¬Ғctitious toy example. They are indeed present in larger neural networks with signiп¬Ғcant implications for their opti- mization. Figure 2 shows the progression of 3-bit quantized weights in a depth-wise separable layer of MobileNetV2 close to convergence, trained using LSQ (Esser et al., 2020) on ImageNet. We observe that many of the weights appear to randomly oscillate between two adjacent quantization levels. In п¬Ғgure 3, we also see that after the supposed convergence of the network, a large fraction of the latent weights lie right at the decision boundary between grid points. This further reinforces the observation that a signiп¬Ғcant proportion of weights oscillate and do not converge. We identify two major issues associated with the oscillations in neural network training: Wrong estimation of the batch- Figure 3.Weight distribution in depth-wise separable layer of third inverted residual block of MobileNetV2 (conv.3.1). Histogram of latent weights (left). Histogram of the distance of latent weight from their closest quantization grid-point (wint вҲ’w/s) (right). normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) inference statistics, and an adverse effect on the optimization of the network. 2.3.1. T HE EFFECT ON BATCH -NORMALIZATION During training batch-normalization layers track the expo- nential moving average (EMA) of the mean and variance for each layerвҖҷs output, so that it can be used during inference as an approximation of the real sample statistics. When training full-precision neural networks we can expect that weights will change very slowly close to convergence. As a result, the output statistics of each layer are expected to be quite stable across iterations and therefore the EMA is a good estimate of the statistics. However, oscillations in QAT can lead to rapid changes of the integer weights (see in п¬Ғgure 2) leading to signiп¬Ғcant distribution shift between iterations, even close to convergence. The sudden and large changes in the output distribution induced by oscillations can corrupt the EMA statistics leading to a signiп¬Ғcant degra- dation in accuracy. In fact, there are two factors that amplify this effect: the weight bit-width and the number of weights per output channel. The lower the bit-width b, the larger the distance becomes between quantization levels, as it is proportional to1/2b. As the oscillating weights move from one quantization level to the other, they cause a proportionally larger shift in the out- put distribution. The second important factor is the numberOvercoming Oscillations in QAT Network Layer max DKL E[DKL] ResNet18 layer1.0.conv1 0.0059 0.0002 ResNet18 layer1.0.conv2 0.0130 0.0014 ResNet18 layer3.0.conv1 0.0006 0.0001 MobileNetV2 conv3.0 (PW) 0.7858 0.0292 MobileNetV2 conv3.1 (DW) 55.3782 1.25464 MobileNetV2 conv3.2 (PW) 0.0065 0.0012 MobileNetV2 conv10.0 (PW) 0.0037 0.0004 MobileNetV2 conv10.1 (DW) 27.2618 0.2900 MobileNetV2 conv10.2 (PW) 0.0267 0.0034 Table 1.Discrepancy between estimated and actual population statistics of batch-normalization following convolutional layers in ResNet18 and MobileNetV2 with weights quantized to 3 bits. We report the maximum and mean KL-divergence across the output channels of each layer. DS: depth-wise separable, PW: point-wise. of weights per output channel. The smaller the number of weights, the larger the contribution of individual weights to the п¬Ғnal accumulation. When the number of accumulations increases, the effects of oscillations average out due to the law of large numbers. In table 1, we use the KL-divergence1 to quantify the dis- crepancy between population and estimated statistics. We indeed observe that KL divergence is much larger for depth- wise separable layers than in point-wise convolutions in MobileNetV2 and the full convolutions in ResNet18. A cheap and straightforward solution to this problem is to re-estimate the batch-normalization statistics with a small subset of data after training. This method, which we call batch-normalization (BN) re-estimation, is sometimes used in stochastic quantization formulations (Peters & Welling, 2018; Louizos et al., 2019). However, we argue that it is also essential in deterministic QAT formulations due to the oscillating weights. In table 2, we see that BN re-estimation not only improves the п¬Ғnal quantized accuracy for MobileNetV2 but also re- duces the variance among different seeds. We further ob- serve that the gap in accuracy widens as the bit-width goes down for MobileNetV2, which is not the case for ResNet18. 2.3.2. T HE EFFECT ON TRAINING Next to harming the BN statistics, oscillations can also have a negative effect on the training process itself. To illustrate this we п¬Ғrst show that a converged MobileNetV2 with 3-bit weights can achieve a lower training loss (and higher valida- 1We we assume that population p and estimated q outputs are normally distributed, such that DKL(p,q) = log Пғ2 2 Пғ2 1 + Пғ2 1+(Вө1вҲ’Вө2)2 2Пғ2 2 вҲ’1 2 , where p(x) = N(Вө1,Пғ1) and q(x) =N(Вө2,Пғ2). Network Bits pre-BN post-BN ResNet18 4 70.150.03 70.200.02 ResNet18 3 69.630.01 69.700.05 MobileNetV2 8 71.790.07 71.890.05 MobileNetV2 4 68.990.44 71.010.05 MobileNetV2 3 64.971.23 69.500.04 Table 2.Validation accuracy (%) on ImageNet before (pre-BN) and after BN re-estimation (post-BN) for networks trained using low bit weight quantization (Esser et al., 2020) for 20 epochs. We report the average of 3 seeds and the STD in the superscript. Method Train Loss Val. Acc. (%) Baseline 1.3566 69.50 SR (mean + std) 1.35470.0053 69.580.09 SR (best) 1.3391 69.85 AdaRound 1.3070 70.12 Freezing - 70.33 Table 3.Ablation study on the effect of oscillations on QAT. Train- ing loss and validation accuracy (%) for MobileNetV2 with 3- bit weight quantization. SR is stochastic rounding of oscillat- ing weights and AdaRound the binary optimization of oscillating weights. tion accuracy) if we randomly sample oscillating weights between their two oscillating states. To this end, we sample all weights that were oscillating at the end of training with a probability proportional to the time spent at each quantized state, i.e. p(wвҶ‘) = Et[wt = wвҶ‘]. We calculate the expec- tation using a exponential moving average over the integer weights as done in line 15 of algorithm 1. In table 3, we present the results of this experiment. We ob- serve that the mean training loss over the sampled networks is similar to that of the п¬Ғnal converged model. However, many samples achieve a lower training loss and the best ran- domly sampled network shows a signiп¬Ғcantly lower training loss. We also perform a binary optimization of the oscil- lating weights using an adaptation of AdaRound (Nagel et al., 2020). We optimize the rounding of all layers si- multaneously on the п¬Ғnal task loss, akin to what is done in the literature with simulated annealing to solve binary optimization problems (Kirkpatrick et al., 1983). We see that this binary optimization signiп¬Ғcantly improves over the best stochastic sample and the original converged network. This suggests that weight oscillations prevent the network from converging to the best local minimum during training, and can be detrimental to the optimization process. Finally, we show that preventing oscillations earlier in train- ing using our oscillation freezing technique (cf. section 4.3) leads to an even higher validation accuracy than the binaryOvercoming Oscillations in QAT optimization of the oscillating weights. This indicates that the oscillations not only prevent QAT from converging to the best local minimum at the end of training, but can also lead the optimizer towards sub-optimal directions earlier in training. 3. Related work Earlier work in QAT (Cai et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2019) investigated different variations of the STE and demonstrate experimentally that the derivative of the clipped ReLU is a better approximation than the vanilla STE (derivative of identity). In practice, the former is the same as setting the gradient outside the quantization grid to zero, as per equation (2), and is the most widely used formulation in QAT literature (Choi et al., 2018; Esser et al., 2020; Uhlich et al., 2020). Despite the success of the STE trick in practice, a lot of prior work has focused on the non-optimality of the STE as a gradient approximator. DВҙefossez et al. (2021) claim that the STE causes instability during training due to weights oscillating leading to biased gradients and weights. For this reason, they propose replacing the rounding operation with additive Gaussian noise in the forward pass to sim- ulate quantization noise. Whereas weight oscillations are indeed an important side-effect of the STE, the idea that the STE yields biased gradients in a deterministic training setting is opaque, as we discuss in more detail in appendix A.4. Nguyen et al. (2020) use a toy example to claim that solutions under STE can lead to a sub-optimal quantized solution but do not recognize that the quantized weight will oscillate around the optimum rather than converge to the wrong value. Based on their observation, they propose an absolute-cosine regularization for the full-precision weights to make them more quantization-friendly but do not com- pare to other competing QAT methods. Other work focuses on alternative gradient estimatorsfor back-propagation. Despite seeming differences in their mo- tivation and formulations, all these methods in on way or another propose adaptations of STE that boil down into two main categories: multiplicative or additive. Multiplica- tive methods apply a scaling to the data-gradient, whereas additive methods add an input-independent term to the gra- dient through regularization. We provide more details and derivations in appendix A.1. Multiplicative J. Lee (2021) (EWGS) acknowledge that the STE generates coarse gradients that treat all latent weights within a quantization bin equally regardless of their distance from its centre and propose scaling the gradient by this distance. However, they do not provide any evidence on why and how these coarse gradient harm optimization. Kim et al. (2020) arrive to a similar formulation but are motivated by robust quantization. They claim that clustering latent weights around bin centres makes them more robust when quantized to different bit widths. In both these methods, the gradient scales linearly with the distance of the latent weight from its quantized value. Gong et al. (2019) (DSQ) try to alleviate the gradient error that the STE causes during low-bit quantization but do not provide any evidence about the source or magnitude of this error. They propose using a series of hyperbolic tangent functions to simulate the rounding function in the backward pass. This leads to a non-linear scaling of the STE gradient, with the gradient increasing with the distance from the bin centre. Yang et al. (2019) also approximate the rounding function with a stack of sigmoid functions to address the gradient mismatchproblem. They parameterize the sigmoid with a temperature, which increases during training to better approximate the step function. As we show in the appendix, these multiplicative methods can affect the magnitude of the oscillations but cannot stop them from happening. In п¬Ғgure 1, we visualize the effect of EWGS and DSQ gradients on the 1D regression problem, described in section 2.2. It is evident, that neither of these methods overcome oscillations. Additive Various regularizations have been proposed in literature to make the weight distribution more quantization- friendly. Chai (2021) apply an L2 regularization on the difference between quantized and п¬Ӯoating-point weights, while Han et al. (2021) apply bin regularization to enforce the weights to cluster at the quantization grid points. Their bin regularization method is similar to our proposed os- cillation dampeningmethod described later in section 4.2. However, their motivation is to form sharper weight distri- bution around the bin centre that reduce the quantization error rather than preventing oscillations. In contrast to the multiplicative methods, these additive methods add a term to the gradient and can thus dampen oscillations by pulling the latent weights towards the grid points. Park & Yoo (2020) recognize that the STE can cause acti- vation instability during training of low-bit quantized Mo- bileNets and propose progressively freezing whole layers during training. Chen et al. (2019) approximate the gradient using a small neural network, whose parameters are learnt using meta-learning but is limited to binary weights. 4. Overcoming oscillations in QAT Now that we have established that oscillations can have a negative effect during optimization, especially for low-bit quantization, we focus on how to overcome them. First we introduce a metric for quantifying oscillations and then we propose two novel techniques aimed at preventing oscilla- tions at their source during quantization-aware training.Overcoming Oscillations in QAT 4.1. Quantifying Oscillations Before we can address the oscillations, we need a way of detecting and measuring them during training. We propose calculating the frequency of oscillations over time using an exponential moving average (EMA). We can then deп¬Ғne a minimum frequency as a threshold for oscillating weights. For an oscillation to occur in iteration tit needs two satisfy two conditions: 1. The integer value of the weight needs to change, thus wt int Мё= wtвҲ’1 int where wt int = clip (вҢҠw s вҢү ,n,p ) is the integer value of the quantized weight. 2. The direction of the change in the integer domain needs to be the opposite than that of its previous change, thus ot = sign(вҲҶt int) Мё= sign(вҲҶП„ int) where П„ is the iteration of the last change in integer domain and вҲҶt int = wt int вҲ’ wtвҲ’1 int , the direction of the change. We then track the frequency of oscillations over time using an exponential moving average (EMA): ft = mВ·ot + (1 вҲ’m) В·ftвҲ’1. (4) 4.2. Oscillation dampening When weights oscillate, they always move around the deci- sion threshold between two quantization bins. This means that oscillating weights are always close to the edge of the quantization bin. In order to dampen the oscillatory behav- ior, we employ a regularization term that encourages latent weights to be close to the center of the bin rather than its edge. We deп¬Ғne our dampening loss similar to weight decay as: Ldampen = вҲҘЛҶw вҲ’clip (w,sn,sp )вҲҘ2 F, (5) where s, nand pare the quantization parameters as deп¬Ғned in equation (1) and ЛҶw are the bin centers. Note, as the bin centers are our optimization target, no gradients propagate back through this term. Our п¬Ғnal training objective is now: L= Ltask + О»Ldampen. We choose to apply our bin regular- ization in the latent weight domain such that the resulting gradient вҲӮLdampen вҲӮw = 2 (w вҲ’ЛҶw) В·1snвүӨwвүӨsp (6) is independent of the scale sand, therefore, indirectly inde- pendent of the bit-width2. We further clip our latent weights to the range of the quantization grid, such that only weights that do not get clipped during quantization receive a reg- ularization effect.
